content.text:
Information Systems Design Methods
Semester:
2  |
Type:
UEF21  |
VHS:
63h  |
Weekly hours:
C: 1h30 / TD: 1h30 / TP: 1h30  |
Personal work VHS:
42h  |
Coefficient:
3  |
Credits:
5
Teaching Objectives
Understand the foundations of enterprise information systems
Master the different IS design methods, their principles and applications
Develop a critical analysis capability of existing methods
Acquire skills to choose and adapt design methods to the context
Become familiar with design tools and technologies
Recommended prior knowledge:
Information systems, software engineering, and databases.
Course Content (Lectures: 21h)
Review of IS Analysis and Design Methods
Definition and components of IS (hardware, software, data, people, processes)
Role of IS in the organisation
Requirements engineering (identification and analysis of user needs, functional and non-functional specification, traceability management)
IS typologies (TPS, MIS, DSS, EIS, KMS, ERP)
IS life cycle models and key development stages
Classical and Systemic Methods
Top-down and bottom-up analysis
Cartesian vs systemic methods
Data and process modelling
MERISE: data models, processing models
Limits of classical and systemic methods
Object-Oriented Methods
Review of OOP fundamentals (encapsulation, inheritance, polymorphism)
UML: use case diagrams, class diagrams, sequence diagrams, etc.
Object-oriented design and development
Implementation and integration of object-oriented systems
Agile Methods
Introduction to agile methods (Scrum, XP, Kanban)
Agile principles and values
Implementing agile methods in IS design
Advantages and disadvantages
Formal Specification and Development Methods
Formal specification methods (B, Event-B, Z)
Formal methods and UML (formal analysis of UML diagrams)
Organisational Aspects and Project Management
IS quality criteria (reliability, performance, security, etc.)
Project management (planning, monitoring, control)
Change management related to new IS implementation
Ethical and social aspects of IS design
Design Tools and Technologies
Modelling tools (CASE, etc.)
Integrated development environments (IDE)
Project management tools
Trends and Challenges in IS Design
Knowledge engineering
Big Data and Artificial Intelligence
Cloud Computing
IS interoperability
Assessment
In-person semester exam (60%)
Continuous assessment (CC) (40%): written tests, personal work
References
Booch, G., Rumbaugh, J., & Jacobson, I. (2005).
The Unified Modeling Language User Guide
. Addison-Wesley.
Nanci, D., et Pascot, D. (1987).
Ingénierie des systèmes d'information: La méthode MERISE
. Editions d'Organisation.
Beck, K. (2000).
Extreme Programming Explained: Embrace Change
. Addison-Wesley.
Schwaber, K., & Beedle, M. (2002).
Agile Software Development with Scrum
. Prentice-Hall.
R. Marciniak, F. Rowe (2009).
Systèmes d'information: dynamique et organisation
. 3e Édition, Economica.
